---
title: "Chapter 20: Budgeting Evaluations"
description: "Detailed study notes for Chapter 20 of Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications."
book_id: "evaluation-theory-models-applications"
collection_id: "evaluation-texts"
note_type: "Chapter"
chapter_number: 20
part: "Part Four: Evaluation Tasks, Procedures, and Tools"
source_pages: "515-539"
citation_key: "stufflebeam_coryn_2014_evaluation_theory_models_applications"
evidence_ids:
  - "chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:ch20-chapter-20-budgeting-evaluations:c0001"
  - "chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:ch20-chapter-20-budgeting-evaluations:c0004"
  - "chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:generic-checklist-for-developing-evaluation-budgets:c0001"
source_status: "needs_review"
---

## Source

<div class="chapter-meta"><span>2014</span><span>Part Four: Evaluation Tasks, Procedures, and Tools</span><span>Chapter 20</span><span>Source pages 515-539</span></div>

This note summarises Chapter 20, "Budgeting Evaluations," from Daniel L. Stufflebeam and Chris L. S. Coryn's *Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications* [@stufflebeam_coryn_2014_evaluation_theory_models_applications]. It is a paraphrased study note based on source-linked MCP research-library retrieval records, not a substitute for the chapter.

The retrieval trail is retained in the source chunks at the bottom of the page. The note paraphrases the chapter's argument and procedures, and it does not reproduce textbook tables, review questions, checklists, exhibits, or extended passages. When a checklist or table is central, the note describes its function and leaves the source artifact in the evidence record.

## Core Argument

Evaluation budgets are ethical and design instruments: they translate the promised scope of work into feasible, transparent, and defensible resource commitments.

## Study Summary

Chapter 20 explains why budgeting is inseparable from evaluation design. A budget should estimate the resources needed to deliver the agreed design and should make assumptions about tasks, personnel, travel, support, timing, indirect costs, and sponsor contributions visible.

The chapter distinguishes fixed and responsive conditions. Preordinate evaluations may support detailed up-front budgets, while responsive evaluations often need general budgets and periodic updating as user needs and interim findings develop.

Ethical budgeting matters. Underbudgeting can make a promised design impossible; padding or hiding costs can undermine trust; vague agreements can produce conflict. The authors treat budgeting as part of professional integrity.

The generic budgeting checklist is meant for constructing and reviewing budgets. It asks evaluators to link cost categories to design tasks, identify the needed level of detail, determine cost factors, and revisit the budget as plans become clearer.

For close reading, track how the chapter uses evaluation budget, line-item budget, modular budget, and cost factor while moving through budget rationale, ethical principles, and illustrative budgets. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.

Within Part Four, this chapter shifts attention from approach selection to execution. It is most useful when read as procedural guidance that still depends on earlier choices about purpose, standards, users, evaluand boundaries, and the kind of value judgment the evaluation must support.

## Key Concepts

- **evaluation budget**: A resource plan that links evaluation tasks, staffing, timing, travel, support, and deliverables to cost.
- **line-item budget**: A budget format that itemizes costs by categories such as personnel, travel, materials, and indirect costs.
- **modular budget**: A budget format that groups costs into larger modules or rounded categories rather than detailed line items.
- **cost factor**: A task, assumption, rate, or condition that drives the cost of evaluation work.
- **grant**: A funding arrangement that supports evaluation or program work under specified sponsor conditions.
- **contract**: A binding agreement that specifies evaluation scope, deliverables, resources, rights, and responsibilities.
- **cooperative agreement**: A funding or working arrangement that involves substantial sponsor and evaluator cooperation.
- **ethical budgeting**: Budgeting that honestly matches promised work to feasible resources and makes uncertainty explicit.

## Chapter Structure

- Budget rationale.
- Ethical principles.
- Illustrative budgets.
- Budget dimensions.
- Generic budgeting checklist.

## Practical Implications

- Build the budget from tasks in the evaluation design.
- Make uncertainty explicit in responsive or evolving evaluations.
- Use the budget as a feasibility test before finalizing promises.

## Concept Map

::: {.concept-map}
```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  Problem["Problem: What value problem does Chapter 20 help evaluators handle?"]
  Central["Concept: Budgeting Evaluations"]
  Concept1["Concept: evaluation budget"]
  Concept2["Concept: line-item budget"]
  Mechanism1["Mechanism: budget rationale"]
  Mechanism2["Mechanism: ethical principles"]
  Implication1["Policy implication: Build the budget from tasks in the evaluation design."]
  Implication2["Policy implication: Make uncertainty explicit in responsive or evolving evaluations."]
  Question["Open question: What evidence would make this guidance credible in a live evaluation?"]

  Problem --> Central
  Central --> Question
  Central --> Concept1
  Central --> Concept2
  Concept1 --> Mechanism1
  Concept2 --> Mechanism2
  Mechanism1 --> Implication1
  Mechanism2 --> Implication2
  Implication1 --> Question

  classDef problem fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d;
  classDef concept fill:#e6f3f1,stroke:#115e59,color:#134e4a;
  classDef mechanism fill:#e8f1ff,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1e3a8a;
  classDef evidence fill:#fff2df,stroke:#b45309,color:#7c2d12;
  classDef implication fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#831843;
  class Problem problem;
  class Central concept;
  class Concept1,Concept2 concept;
  class Mechanism1,Mechanism2 mechanism;
  class Implication1,Implication2,Question implication;
```
:::

## Connections

- Book overview: [Overview](index.qmd)
- Reading route: [Chapter Map](chapter-map.qmd)
- Concepts synthesis: [Core Concepts](concepts.qmd)
- Practice synthesis: [Practice Implications](practice-implications.qmd)
- Previous: [Chapter 19: Designing Evaluations](chapter-19-designing-evaluations.qmd)
- Next: [Chapter 21: Contracting Evaluations](chapter-21-contracting-evaluations.qmd)

## Study Prompts

1. What value problem does Chapter 20 help an evaluator diagnose?
2. Which assumptions would need evidence before applying this chapter in a live evaluation?
3. How would this chapter change the way an evaluator scopes questions, methods, reporting, or use?
4. Where could the guidance be misused if treated as a fixed template?

## Source Chunks

<details class="source-chunks">
<summary>Source chunks</summary>
<ul class="source-chunk-list">
<li><code>chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:ch20-chapter-20-budgeting-evaluations:c0001</code></li>
<li><code>chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:ch20-chapter-20-budgeting-evaluations:c0004</code></li>
<li><code>chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:generic-checklist-for-developing-evaluation-budgets:c0001</code></li>
</ul>
</details>

## References

- Stufflebeam, D. L., & Coryn, C. L. S. (2014). *Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications*. Jossey-Bass. Chapter 20, pages 515-539.
- Saved retrieval metadata: `data/research-library/evidence/evaluation-theory-models-applications/chapter-evidence-summary.json`.
